title: Filter with WHERE — find the engineers
instruction:
The `WHERE` clause filters rows before they're returned. Every column in the table can be a filter.

Find all employees in the Engineering department (department_id = 1).
starter:
SELECT name, job_title, salary
FROM employees
WHERE department_id = 1
ORDER BY salary DESC;
successMessage: 5 engineers, from VP to Junior. Notice the salary range: $92k to $185k. In the next step you'll filter by salary range using `BETWEEN` to find the mid-level engineers.

title: BETWEEN — salary band analysis
instruction:
`BETWEEN x AND y` filters rows where a value falls in a range (inclusive). This is cleaner than writing `salary >= 100000 AND salary <= 150000`.

Find all employees earning between $100k and $150k — the mid-to-senior band.
starter:
SELECT name, job_title, salary,
       ROUND(salary / 1000.0, 0) AS salary_k
FROM employees
WHERE salary BETWEEN 100000 AND 150000
ORDER BY salary DESC;
successMessage: 7 employees in the $100k–$150k band. The `ROUND(salary / 1000.0, 0)` expression computes a derived column — you can do math directly in SELECT without storing the result.

title: IN — filter across multiple values
instruction:
`IN (v1, v2, v3)` is cleaner than writing `OR` for each value. It reads naturally and scales well.

Find all customers from English-speaking countries.
starter:
SELECT name, country, joined_date
FROM customers
WHERE country IN ('USA', 'UK', 'Canada', 'Australia')
ORDER BY joined_date;
successMessage: 9 of 10 customers are from English-speaking countries. `NOT IN (...)` would give you the inverse — non-English-speaking countries. Both are cleaner than chaining multiple OR conditions.

title: LIKE — search by partial text
instruction:
`LIKE` matches patterns in text. `%` matches any sequence of characters. It's how you build search features.

Find all employees whose title contains "Engineer" or whose name starts with specific letters.
starter:
SELECT name, job_title, salary
FROM employees
WHERE job_title LIKE '%Engineer%'
ORDER BY salary DESC;
successMessage: `'%Engineer%'` matches any title containing the word Engineer anywhere — VP Engineering, Senior Engineer, DevOps Engineer, etc. Case sensitivity depends on your database (SQLite is case-insensitive by default for ASCII).
